analysis.problem: 839 million people live in poverty globally, with an increase in extreme poverty rate in Sub-Saharan Africa
analysis.root_cause: Inequality and lack of economic growth in poor regions, exacerbated by ineffective welfare programs and capitalist systems that reinforce generational poverty
leverage_points: International organizations such as the World Bank and United Nations Development Programme
Governments of countries with high poverty rates

proposed_actions.action: Implement targeted poverty reduction programs, such as microfinance initiatives and vocational training
proposed_actions.measurable_goal: Reduce poverty rates by 10% in the next 2 years

proposed_actions.action: Promote policies that address income inequality, such as progressive taxation and social welfare programs
proposed_actions.measurable_goal: Decrease the Gini coefficient by 5% in the next 5 years

evidence: The World Bank reports that 839 million people lived in poverty in 2024
Income inequality trends in Sub-Saharan Africa show a divergence in economic growth and poverty rates
The Multidimensional Poverty Index for Latin America reveals sticky deprivations and slow progress in reducing poverty
analysis.generalization: This problem connects to the domains of PLANET (resource distribution), HUMAN (well-being and dignity), CIVILIZATION (economic systems and governance), and COSMOS (long-term sustainability and global cooperation)
analysis.summary: Poverty affects nearly 1 billion people worldwide, with increasing rates in some regions, and requires targeted and sustained efforts to reduce inequality and promote economic growth. International cooperation and effective governance are crucial to addressing this complex problem.

raw_items.title: Inequality and the impact of growth on poverty in sub‐Saharan Africa: A GMM estimator in a dynamic panel threshold model - ScienceDirect
raw_items.summary: September 25, 2023 - The need for a good understanding of the relationship among poverty, inequality, and economic growth has become a significant concern in poor countries. This paper, therefore, empirically analyzes the triangular relationship among extreme poverty, income inequality, and economic growth for the 45 middle and low‐income sub‐Saharan African countries over the period 2010–2021.

raw_items.title: Nine key facts about poverty and inequality in Latin America
raw_items.summary: July 16, 2024 - GDP in Latin America & the Caribbean will expand by 1.6% in 2024 and a growth of 2.7% and 2.6% are expected for 2025 and 2026. These rates are the lowest compared to all other regions in the world, and insufficient to drive prosperity.
